attributes.stem:
A 21-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a five-month history of pruritic rash on the soles of his feet associated with an unpleasant smell. He works as a construction worker and wears steel-capped boots all day for work. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple small, punched-out pits on the pressure-bearing areas of both soles. The lesions are non-tender. His feet are notably sweaty with wet woollen socks, and there is a distinctive malodour. The rest of the skin examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of pitted keratolysis: multiple small, punched-out pits on the weight-bearing areas of the soles, associated with malodour. The condition is caused by bacterial overgrowth (particularly Corynebacterium species) in the setting of hyperhidrosis and prolonged occlusion from footwear. It can also present with mildly itchy brown rash (from pits in the stratum corneum) and can also occur on the palms of the hands.

Pitted keratolysis typically affects people who wear occlusive, non-breathable or misfitting footwear for prolonged periods. The pits are caused by bacterial exoenzymes that break down the stratum corneum. The characteristic malodour is due to sulfur compounds produced by the bacteria. It is typically a clinical diagnosis. Management is with antiperspirant to the feet, rotating good quality socks to prevent dampness and may require topical antibiotics. It usually responds well to treatment within 3 - 4 weeks.

Plantar warts (verrucae) appear as well-circumscribed hyperkeratotic lesions with tiny black dots (thrombosed capillaries) and interrupted skin lines. They are typically painful on lateral compression and do not produce malodour.

Tinea pedis typically presents with scaling, maceration, and pruritus, particularly in the interdigital spaces. While it can have an unpleasant smell, it does not cause pitting of the skin which is characteristic of pitted keratolysis.

Pompholyx (dyshidrotic eczema) presents with intensely pruritic vesicles on the palms and soles, which may evolve into scaling and fissuring. It does not cause pitting or malodour.

Palmoplantar hyperhidrosis is characterised by excessive sweating of the palms and soles. While it can predispose to pitted keratolysis, it does not itself cause pitting of the skin. The sweating may be associated with malodour but this is typically less pronounced than in pitted keratolysis.
learningPoints: Pitted keratolysis presents with characteristic small, punched-out pits on the weight-bearing areas of the soles ad malodour.
The condition is caused by bacterial overgrowth, particularly Corynebacterium species, in warm and moist environments.
references: Lam C, Mitchell G. Pitted keratolysis. DermNet Nz. 2022. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/pitted-keratolysis
Morais P.  Smelly foot rash. Australian Family Physician. 2011: 40 (3). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/4e7115d8-019b-4719-98b1-6f8ebf484a4e/Smelly-foot-rash.aspx
Saravanan R, Baalan K. Pitted Keratloysis. Pan Africal Medical Journal. 2022.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9250681/
